meanings.definition: To officially establish, order, or decree something by law, authority, or divine will.
meanings.example: The constitution ordains that every citizen is entitled to a fair and speedy trial.

meanings.definition: To officially confer holy orders upon someone, appointing them as a priest, minister, or rabbi.
meanings.example: She was ordained as a minister after completing six years of theological training.

meanings.definition: To gain a strategic or competitive advantage over an opponent through superior tactics, skill, or planning.
meanings.example: The nimble startup outmanoeuvred its larger rivals by targeting niche markets they had overlooked.

meanings.definition: To defeat an opponent in military conflict by executing superior tactical movements.
meanings.example: The general outmanoeuvred the enemy by feigning retreat and then encircling them from the rear.

meanings.definition: Of greater importance or influence than all other factors; taking precedence over everything else.
meanings.example: The board's overriding concern throughout the crisis was preserving the company's financial stability.

meanings.definition: Exerting a prevailing or dominant influence that supersedes other considerations.
meanings.example: The overriding theme of the summit was the urgent need for multilateral action on climate change.

meanings.definition: Behaving in an excessively controlling, domineering, or arrogant manner toward others.
meanings.example: His overbearing management style stifled creativity and led to unusually high staff turnover.

meanings.definition: Overwhelming in force, intensity, or effect so as to suppress all opposition.
meanings.example: The overbearing heat of the desert made any outdoor exertion hazardous during the midday hours.

meanings.definition: Occurring immediately before the last item in a sequence, series, or ranking.
meanings.example: In the penultimate chapter, the author finally reveals the identity of the elusive antagonist.

meanings.definition: Relating to or denoting the second-to-last syllable of a word.
meanings.example: In classical Latin, stress regularly falls on the penultimate syllable when it is long.

meanings.definition: Insisting on immediate obedience or compliance and not open to any discussion or refusal.
meanings.example: She received a peremptory summons to appear before the disciplinary committee without delay.

meanings.definition: Of a person or their manner: imperious and expecting immediate compliance without argument.
meanings.example: His peremptory dismissal of every objection made further negotiation impossible.

meanings.definition: Never ending or changing; continuing for an unlimited or very long period.
meanings.example: The Arctic explorer had to adapt to perpetual daylight during the summer months.

meanings.definition: Occurring repeatedly so as to seem endless or incessant.
meanings.example: The perpetual bickering between the two departments was undermining the organisation's morale.

meanings.definition: Causing anxiety, unease, or disquiet; troubling or disturbing to the mind.
meanings.example: The auditors found several perturbing discrepancies in the company's financial records.

meanings.definition: Causing a deviation from a normal or expected course or condition.
meanings.example: The perturbing influence of a nearby mass caused measurable deviations in the spacecraft's trajectory.

meanings.definition: To make an earnest, emotional, or desperate request or appeal to someone.
meanings.example: She pleaded with the court to show leniency given her client's otherwise unblemished record.

meanings.definition: To formally state one's position or enter a plea in a court of law.
meanings.example: The defendant chose to plead not guilty and exercised his right to a jury trial.

meanings.definition: To put forward something as a reason or excuse for one's actions.
meanings.example: He could not plead ignorance of the regulations, as he had signed the compliance form.

meanings.definition: The division of a group or society into two sharply contrasting or opposed factions.
meanings.example: The referendum accelerated the political polarisation of the country into two irreconcilable camps.

meanings.definition: The process by which electromagnetic waves are constrained to vibrate in a particular direction.
meanings.example: Polarisation filters are used in photography to reduce glare from reflective surfaces.

meanings.definition: Taken as a precaution; carried out to prevent possible harm, danger, or negative outcomes.
meanings.example: The government issued a precautionary recall of the product pending further safety investigations.

meanings.definition: Based on the precautionary principle, acting in advance of scientific certainty to avoid harm.
meanings.example: The precautionary measures imposed on GMO crops reflect regulatory caution in the face of scientific uncertainty.

meanings.definition: Surpassing all others; having paramount importance, influence, or distinction in a field.
meanings.example: She became the preeminent authority on Byzantine art history through decades of dedicated scholarship.

meanings.definition: A state of severe deprivation of basic necessities such as food, warmth, or shelter.
meanings.example: The refugees endured months of privation in makeshift camps before aid finally reached them.

meanings.definition: The absence or removal of a quality, condition, or something previously possessed.
meanings.example: In classical philosophy, darkness is often defined as the privation of light rather than a substance in itself.

meanings.definition: A high degree of competence, skill, or expertise in a particular subject or activity.
meanings.example: Applicants must demonstrate proficiency in at least two programming languages to be considered for the role.

meanings.definition: The ability to perform a task efficiently and with little effort due to practice or experience.
meanings.example: Her proficiency at navigating complex legal documents saved the team considerable time.

meanings.definition: Advocating or relating to social or political reform and the adoption of more modern ideas.
meanings.example: The party adopted a progressive platform emphasising equality, environmental protection, and expanded public services.

meanings.definition: Happening or developing gradually over a period of time; occurring in stages.
meanings.example: The progressive deterioration of the patient's vision required increasingly frequent specialist reviews.

meanings.definition: Using new or innovative methods; favouring change and improvement.
meanings.example: The school's progressive teaching methods placed collaborative problem-solving above rote memorisation.

meanings.definition: The action or process of calculating or estimating something.
meanings.example: By her reckoning, the project would take at least another three months to complete.

meanings.definition: A time when the consequences of past actions or mistakes must finally be faced.
meanings.example: The financial crisis was seen as a day of reckoning for banks that had taken excessive risks.

meanings.definition: The determination of a ship's or aircraft's position based on known speed, heading, and elapsed time.
meanings.example: Dead reckoning was the primary method of navigation before the advent of GPS technology.

meanings.definition: Unwillingness or hesitation to do something; a state of being disinclined.
meanings.example: Despite his obvious reluctance, he agreed to testify before the parliamentary inquiry.

meanings.definition: A measure of opposition to the flow of magnetic flux through a magnetic circuit.
meanings.example: The reluctance of the magnetic core determines the efficiency of the transformer's energy transfer.

meanings.definition: A solution or means of counteracting or eliminating a problem, fault, or difficulty.
meanings.example: The government proposed several economic remedies to address rising unemployment.

meanings.definition: A medicine, treatment, or therapy used to cure a disease or relieve symptoms.
meanings.example: The herbalist recommended a natural remedy involving ginger and honey for the persistent cough.

meanings.definition: A legal means by which a wrong or injury is redressed or compensated in a court of law.
meanings.example: The claimant sought a legal remedy in the form of damages and a formal injunction.

meanings.definition: A temporary postponement or cancellation of a punishment, especially a death sentence.
meanings.example: The condemned prisoner received a last-minute reprieve when new evidence cast doubt on the conviction.

meanings.definition: A temporary relief from an unpleasant or difficult situation.
meanings.example: The unexpected funding offered a brief reprieve for the charity, which had been on the verge of closure.

meanings.definition: Remaining after the greater part has been removed, used, or dealt with.
meanings.example: Residual traces of the pesticide were detected in the groundwater months after application had ceased.

meanings.definition: Relating to the residual value of an asset or the remainder in a statistical model.
meanings.example: The accountants calculated the residual value of the equipment at the end of its useful life.

meanings.definition: Presenting information in a way that is intended to provoke excitement, shock, or strong emotion rather than inform accurately.
meanings.example: Critics accused the tabloid of sensationalist reporting that distorted the facts of the case.

meanings.definition: Relating to or advocating the philosophical doctrine that all knowledge derives from sensory experience.
meanings.example: The sensationalist philosophers of the Enlightenment argued that the mind begins as a blank slate.

meanings.definition: A military operation in which forces surround and blockade a fortified place to compel its surrender.
meanings.example: The city withstood a siege of nearly six months before supply shortages finally forced negotiations.

meanings.definition: A prolonged period of intense difficulty, pressure, or hardship.
meanings.example: The company endured a siege of negative press coverage following the product recall.

meanings.definition: Unity or agreement of feeling, action, or interest among individuals in a group; mutual support.
meanings.example: Workers expressed their solidarity by staging a one-day strike in support of their dismissed colleagues.

meanings.definition: A sense of cohesion within a community or group based on shared goals or circumstances.
meanings.example: The crisis fostered a spirit of solidarity among neighbours who had previously had little contact.

meanings.definition: An assumption or hypothesis that is accepted without proof as the basis for reasoning.
meanings.example: His entire argument rested on the supposition that all rational agents will act in their own interest.

meanings.definition: A thing assumed or believed to be true without direct evidence.
meanings.example: The attribution of the manuscript to Shakespeare remains mere supposition in the absence of documentary proof.

meanings.definition: Serving as a symbol; representing or expressing something abstract through an object, person, or event.
meanings.example: The handshake between the two leaders was largely symbolic but signalled a significant diplomatic thaw.

meanings.definition: Relating to or employing the use of symbols, especially in literature, art, or religious ritual.
meanings.example: The painting is rich with symbolic imagery drawn from classical mythology and Christian iconography.

meanings.definition: Done or acting according to a fixed, orderly plan or system; methodical.
meanings.example: The inspector conducted a systematic review of all safety procedures across every department.

meanings.definition: Relating to a system or systems as a whole rather than to individual components.
meanings.example: The researchers argued that poverty is a systematic problem requiring structural solutions, not individual charity.

meanings.definition: In a manner that is understood or implied without being directly stated; without words.
meanings.example: By continuing to use the platform, users tacitly accept its terms and conditions.

meanings.definition: In a silent or uncommunicative manner; without expressing something openly.
meanings.example: She tacitly acknowledged the error by quietly correcting the document without drawing attention to the mistake.

meanings.definition: Equivalent in seriousness, effect, or meaning to something else; virtually the same as.
meanings.example: Refusing to cooperate with the investigation would be tantamount to an admission of guilt.

meanings.definition: Too long, slow, or dull; causing tedium through excessive repetition or lack of variety.
meanings.example: The auditing process was tedious but necessary to ensure full regulatory compliance.

meanings.definition: Proceeding at a painfully slow pace; dragging out beyond reasonable duration.
meanings.example: After a tedious eight-hour hearing, the tribunal adjourned without reaching a verdict.

meanings.definition: Characterised by strong, turbulent, or conflicting emotions; marked by turmoil or passion.
meanings.example: Their tempestuous relationship alternated between fierce arguments and intense reconciliations.

meanings.definition: Relating to or resembling a storm; very windy, stormy, or violent.
meanings.example: The tempestuous conditions in the North Sea forced the vessels to seek shelter in a nearby harbour.

meanings.definition: Showing or suffering from nervousness, fear, or a lack of confidence; easily frightened.
meanings.example: The timorous witness could barely be heard as he gave his account of the incident.

meanings.definition: Lacking boldness or courage in expression or action; excessively tentative.
meanings.example: The committee's timorous response to the crisis was widely criticised as inadequate and indecisive.

meanings.definition: Holding or constituting a purely nominal or ceremonial title without exercising real power.
meanings.example: As a constitutional monarch, she is the titular head of state but has no executive authority.

meanings.definition: Relating to or denoting the character, person, or institution from which a book, film, or similar work takes its title.
meanings.example: The titular character of the novel does not appear until the final third of the narrative.

meanings.definition: Not open to more than one interpretation; clear and decisive in meaning.
meanings.example: The contract language must be unambiguous to avoid future disputes over the parties' obligations.

meanings.definition: Leaving no doubt; expressing something in a clear and definite manner.
meanings.example: The CEO gave an unambiguous statement that no further redundancies were planned.

meanings.definition: To estimate something as being smaller, less, or less significant than it actually is.
meanings.example: Project managers often underestimate the time required for integration testing in complex systems.

meanings.definition: To regard someone as less capable, intelligent, or powerful than they actually are.
meanings.example: Rival teams consistently underestimated her strategic ability, which proved to be a costly mistake.

meanings.definition: Having no foundation in fact; not based on evidence or sound reasoning.
meanings.example: The allegations against the charity were found to be entirely unfounded following a thorough investigation.

meanings.definition: Not acting in accordance with moral principles; having no ethical standards.
meanings.example: The unprincipled lobbyist exploited regulatory loopholes for the benefit of his corporate clients.

meanings.definition: Not supported by evidence or proof; lacking factual corroboration.
meanings.example: The news outlet retracted the story after its central claims proved to be unsubstantiated.

meanings.definition: The display of strong feeling; forceful energy, passion, or intensity.
meanings.example: She spoke with such vehemence that the entire audience fell silent.

meanings.definition: Great force, vigour, or urgency in expression or action.
meanings.example: The union leader's vehemence caught senior management off guard during the negotiations.

meanings.definition: The action of clearing someone of blame or suspicion; justification by proof.
meanings.example: The acquittal was seen as a complete vindication of the scientist, whose methodology had been harshly criticised.

meanings.definition: The act of providing justification or support for a theory, claim, or decision.
meanings.example: The discovery of new archaeological evidence was treated as a vindication of her controversial hypothesis.

meanings.definition: To remain undamaged, unaffected, or unmoved by (a force or adversity); to endure.
meanings.example: The bridge was engineered to withstand seismic activity well above the historical maximum for the region.

meanings.definition: To oppose or resist (a person, force, or pressure) successfully.
meanings.example: The coalition withstood enormous political pressure to abandon its environmental commitments.

meanings.definition: In a manner showing great energy, enthusiasm, and dedication in pursuit of a cause or goal.
meanings.example: The charity's volunteers zealously campaigned for legislative change throughout the electoral cycle.

meanings.definition: The state or quality of being abnormal; a deviation from what is standard, typical, or expected.
meanings.example: The scan revealed a small abnormality in the tissue that required further investigation.

meanings.definition: A feature or characteristic that deviates from the normal or expected standard.
meanings.example: Researchers catalogued a series of genetic abnormalities linked to the rare metabolic disorder.

meanings.definition: The quality or state of being ridiculously unreasonable, illogical, or inappropriate.
meanings.example: He pointed out the absurdity of a policy that penalised workers for taking sick leave.

meanings.definition: A statement, action, or idea that is absurd; something wildly unreasonable or incongruous.
meanings.example: The play luxuriates in the absurdities of bureaucratic life, producing both laughter and unease.

meanings.definition: The act of recognising the existence, truth, or validity of something; acceptance of a fact.
meanings.example: Her public acknowledgement of the error restored confidence among the organisation's stakeholders.

meanings.definition: An expression of gratitude or appreciation for a contribution or assistance.
meanings.example: The book's acknowledgement section lists over two dozen colleagues who contributed to the research.

meanings.definition: A formal confirmation that a message, letter, or request has been received.
meanings.example: Please send an acknowledgement of receipt once you have reviewed the attached documents.

meanings.definition: The state of existing in reality; the actual conditions or facts of a situation.
meanings.example: In actuality, the reforms produced far fewer savings than the government had initially projected.

meanings.definition: Something that is real rather than imagined or hypothetical; an existing fact.
meanings.example: The philosopher distinguished between possibilities and actualities in his theory of modal logic.

meanings.definition: Skill and cleverness in using the hands or mind; dexterity and ingenuity.
meanings.example: The negotiator's adroitness in steering between competing interests earned her wide respect.

meanings.definition: The arrangement of things in a straight line or in correct relative positions.
meanings.example: Regular checks ensure the alignment of the machinery components and prevent premature wear.

meanings.definition: A position of agreement or alliance among people, groups, or principles.
meanings.example: The two parties achieved alignment on fiscal policy, paving the way for a coalition government.

meanings.definition: The process of adjusting something so that it corresponds or is in harmony with something else.
meanings.example: The new strategy required alignment of the company's culture with its stated values.

meanings.definition: An item of additional material, typically a section added to the end of a book or other publication.
meanings.example: The addendum to the report contained updated figures that became available after the original submission.

meanings.definition: Something that is added or appended to a contract, agreement, or document to modify or supplement it.
meanings.example: Both parties signed an addendum extending the lease by two years and adjusting the rental terms.

meanings.definition: A claim or assertion that someone has done something illegal or wrong, typically one made without proof.
meanings.example: The company vigorously denied the allegations of financial misconduct published in the press.

meanings.definition: A formal statement of a fact in legal proceedings that the party undertakes to prove.
meanings.example: The prosecution listed the allegations clearly in the indictment before presenting evidence to the jury.

meanings.definition: To increase the volume, strength, or intensity of (a sound, signal, or effect).
meanings.example: The microphone was used to amplify her voice so that it could be heard throughout the auditorium.

meanings.definition: To make (something) more pronounced, detailed, or widely disseminated.
meanings.example: Social media platforms can amplify misinformation at a scale impossible in earlier eras of communication.

meanings.definition: To add detail or further explanation to a statement or account.
meanings.example: The witness was asked to amplify her earlier testimony with specific dates and locations.

meanings.definition: To add notes or comments to a text, document, diagram, or other material to provide explanation or critical commentary.
meanings.example: Students were asked to annotate the primary source documents using specific analytical frameworks.

meanings.definition: To add metadata or labels to data, images, or code in order to categorise or describe content.
meanings.example: The team spent weeks annotating training data before the machine learning model could be developed.

meanings.definition: Directly opposed or in sharp contrast to something; constituting an antithesis.
meanings.example: His actions were antithetical to the principles of transparency that the organisation had publicly espoused.

meanings.definition: To reach an authoritative judgment or decision in a dispute as an independent third party.
meanings.example: Both parties agreed to arbitrate their contractual dispute rather than pursue costly litigation.

meanings.definition: To act as an arbitrator or umpire in a competition or conflict.
meanings.example: An international panel was appointed to arbitrate the boundary dispute between the two nations.

meanings.definition: Favouring or enforcing strict obedience to authority at the expense of personal freedom; characterised by centralised control.
meanings.example: The regime became increasingly authoritarian, censoring the press and imprisoning political opponents.

meanings.definition: Of a person: demanding unquestioning obedience from others; autocratic.
meanings.example: His authoritarian management style created an atmosphere of fear that stifled innovation.

meanings.definition: Involving two parties, especially two nations or organizations, each making commitments to the other.
meanings.example: The two governments signed a bilateral trade agreement to reduce tariffs and streamline customs procedures.

meanings.definition: Relating to or having two sides; affecting both sides of something.
meanings.example: The patient presented with bilateral hearing loss that required fitting of aids in both ears.

meanings.definition: To formally renounce or give up (a throne, authority, or a high office or responsibility).
meanings.example: The king abdicated in favour of his eldest daughter following the constitutional crisis.

meanings.definition: To fail to fulfil or undertake a duty or responsibility; to abandon an obligation.
meanings.example: By ignoring the crisis, the government was accused of abdicating its responsibility to protect citizens.

meanings.definition: A political system in which the ruler holds unlimited and unrestrained power; the principle of absolute government.
meanings.example: The revolution was partly a reaction against centuries of royal absolutism that had suppressed individual freedoms.

meanings.definition: The acceptance of or belief in absolute principles in political, philosophical, or theological matters.
meanings.example: Moral absolutism holds that certain actions are intrinsically right or wrong regardless of context or consequences.

meanings.definition: The formal act of refraining from voting in a legislative or decision-making body.
meanings.example: Three members of the council declared abstentions rather than vote for or against the controversial proposal.

meanings.definition: The practice of restraining oneself from indulging in something, especially alcohol or certain foods.
meanings.example: His doctor recommended abstention from alcohol during the six-week course of medication.

meanings.definition: Expressing agreement or assent; asserting that something is true or valid.
meanings.example: The committee gave an affirmative response to the proposal, allowing the project to proceed to the next stage.

meanings.definition: Favoring or relating to policies that seek to correct the effects of historical discrimination.
meanings.example: The university introduced affirmative measures to increase the representation of underrepresented groups.

meanings.definition: A decrease in size or wasting away of a body part or tissue from disuse or disease.
meanings.example: After months of immobility following the accident, the patient experienced significant muscle atrophy in the injured limb.

meanings.definition: A gradual decline in effectiveness, strength, or vitality due to neglect or lack of use.
meanings.example: Without regular practice, even advanced language skills can atrophy over time.

meanings.definition: A system of government or administration in which most decisions are made by appointed officials following rigid procedures rather than by elected representatives.
meanings.example: Critics argued that excessive bureaucracy was strangling innovation and slowing the delivery of public services.

meanings.definition: Excessively complicated administrative procedure associated with official business.
meanings.example: Starting a small business requires navigating considerable bureaucracy, including multiple licences and registrations.

meanings.definition: The action of surrendering or ceasing to resist an opponent or unwelcome demand; the act of giving in.
meanings.example: The government's capitulation to the protesters' demands was seen as a sign of political weakness.

meanings.definition: The formal surrender of a city, fortress, or army on stipulated terms.
meanings.example: The capitulation of the besieged garrison ended six months of intense fighting.

meanings.definition: To formally give up, surrender, or transfer (territory, rights, or power) to another party.
meanings.example: Under the treaty, the defeated nation was forced to cede several territories to its neighbour.

meanings.definition: To yield or concede ground in an argument or negotiation.
meanings.example: After hours of debate, the committee chair finally ceded the point and agreed to revise the proposal.

meanings.definition: To arrange (laws, rules, or principles) systematically into a code or unified body.
meanings.example: The government appointed a commission to codify the existing criminal statutes into a single comprehensive code.

meanings.definition: To arrange or systematise principles, practices, or beliefs into a fixed and authoritative form.
meanings.example: The association worked to codify best practices into a set of professional standards for the industry.

meanings.definition: Used in ordinary or familiar conversation; not formal or literary in style.
meanings.example: The report was criticised for its colloquial language, which was deemed inappropriate for an official document.

meanings.definition: Characteristic of informal spoken language, often varying by region or social group.
meanings.example: Several colloquial expressions in the original text had to be replaced with more neutral equivalents in the translation.

meanings.definition: Serving to commemorate a person, event, or occasion; intended to preserve the memory of something.
meanings.example: A commemorative plaque was unveiled outside the building to mark the centenary of its founder's birth.

meanings.definition: Relating to an official event, publication, or object created to mark a significant anniversary or milestone.
meanings.example: The post office issued a commemorative stamp series to celebrate fifty years of the national space programme.

meanings.definition: To deliver or transfer something formally to someone's custody, care, or possession.
meanings.example: The documents were consigned to the national archive for permanent preservation.

meanings.definition: To assign someone or something to a particular undesirable fate or situation.
meanings.example: Generations of social policy had effectively consigned the poorest communities to permanent disadvantage.

meanings.definition: To assert something as a position in an argument; to maintain or argue a point.
meanings.example: The defence team contended that the evidence had been improperly obtained and should be excluded.

meanings.definition: To struggle to surmount a difficulty or to compete with others for an objective.
meanings.example: The charity had to contend with a dramatic reduction in funding while demand for its services increased.

meanings.definition: Mutually inconsistent or in direct opposition to each other; involving contradictions.
meanings.example: The two witnesses gave contradictory accounts of the events, complicating the investigation.

meanings.definition: Of a person: inclined to deny or oppose what others say.
meanings.example: His contradictory nature made it difficult to reach agreement even on straightforward matters.

meanings.definition: To call people together for a formal meeting, assembly, or official gathering.
meanings.example: The secretary-general convoked an emergency session of the assembly to address the unfolding humanitarian crisis.

meanings.definition: To establish a mutual relationship or connection between two or more things; to show a correspondence.
meanings.example: Researchers found that high levels of air pollution correlated strongly with increased rates of respiratory illness.

meanings.definition: To have a mutual relationship or connection; to be analogous to something else.
meanings.example: Investment in education correlates with long-term economic growth in most countries studied.

meanings.definition: Made in imitation of something genuine, with the intent to deceive or defraud; forged.
meanings.example: Customs officials seized a large consignment of counterfeit designer goods at the port of entry.

meanings.definition: Assuming a false appearance; pretended or insincere.
meanings.example: His expression of concern was entirely counterfeit — he had known about the problem for weeks.

meanings.definition: Failure to fulfil an obligation, especially a financial one such as the repayment of a loan.
meanings.example: The company went into default after failing to make three consecutive payments to its bondholders.

meanings.definition: A pre-selected option adopted by a computer program or device when no specific alternative is indicated.
meanings.example: The application uses English as its default language unless the user specifies a different preference.

meanings.definition: The outcome that results from inaction or absence of a decision.
meanings.example: By failing to respond to the invitation, she won the position by default when all other candidates withdrew.

meanings.definition: To put off (an action or event) to a later time; to postpone.
meanings.example: The board voted to defer the final decision until a full financial audit had been completed.

meanings.definition: To yield respectfully to the opinion, wishes, or authority of another person.
meanings.example: She deferred to her more experienced colleague on matters of clinical diagnosis.

meanings.definition: To determine or establish the limits or boundaries of something; to demarcate.
meanings.example: The committee was tasked with delimiting the scope of the investigation to ensure it remained focused.

meanings.definition: To separate or mark off distinct elements within a data structure using a specific character or symbol.
meanings.example: The data fields are delimited by commas in the CSV file, making them easy to import into the database.

meanings.definition: To represent or show (something) in a picture, painting, or other artistic medium.
meanings.example: The mural depicts scenes from the city's industrial history, celebrating the lives of ordinary workers.

meanings.definition: To describe or portray something in words or other narrative form.
meanings.example: The documentary depicts the humanitarian crisis with unflinching candour and moral clarity.

meanings.definition: To officially appoint or assign someone to a particular role, task, or position.
meanings.example: The board designated her as the interim chief executive pending a formal recruitment process.

meanings.definition: To mark or identify something for a specific purpose or classification.
meanings.example: Several areas along the coastline were designated as marine conservation zones.

meanings.definition: To reduce the value, quality, or importance of something; to diminish the worth of something.
meanings.example: The minor factual errors in the report do not significantly detract from the strength of its core argument.

meanings.definition: To speak critically of something or someone; to belittle.
meanings.example: Political opponents tried to detract from the policy's achievements by focusing on its implementation delays.

meanings.definition: Entirely lacking or free from a specified quality or characteristic.
meanings.example: The official response to the disaster was devoid of empathy and left many survivors feeling abandoned.

meanings.definition: Of or typical of a ruler with total, absolute power; autocratic and oppressive.
meanings.example: The president's dictatorial tendencies became clearer as he dissolved parliament and suspended elections.

meanings.definition: Issuing commands in an arrogant, overbearing manner; imperious.
meanings.example: Her dictatorial tone alienated colleagues who might otherwise have supported the initiative.

meanings.definition: To take apart a machine, structure, or system piece by piece.
meanings.example: The decommissioned nuclear plant will take decades to safely dismantle and decontaminate.

meanings.definition: To systematically demolish, break down, or render ineffective (an organisation, system, or structure).
meanings.example: The incoming administration promised to dismantle the regulatory framework built up over the previous decade.

meanings.definition: Believing in or based on the principle that all people are equal and deserve equal rights and opportunities.
meanings.example: The cooperative adopted an egalitarian pay structure in which no employee earned more than ten times the lowest salary.

meanings.definition: Characterised by a lack of hierarchy or special privileges; equal in treatment or access.
meanings.example: The new platform claimed to offer a genuinely egalitarian space for political debate, free from editorial bias.

meanings.definition: To make someone more confident or daring; to give someone courage to act or speak.
meanings.example: The early success of their campaign emboldened the activists to escalate their demands.

meanings.definition: Based on, concerned with, or verifiable by observation or experience rather than theory or pure logic.
meanings.example: The researchers sought to demonstrate empirically that the intervention improved long-term health outcomes.

meanings.definition: In practice or through actual experience, as opposed to what is prescribed in theory.
meanings.example: Empirically, the new software proved far less stable than its developers had projected.

meanings.definition: To compel compliance with (a law, rule, or obligation); to put into effect by authority.
meanings.example: The regulator has the power to enforce compliance and impose substantial fines for violations.

meanings.definition: To impose or cause to happen by force or compulsion.
meanings.example: The peacekeeping mission was tasked with enforcing the ceasefire agreement between the warring factions.

meanings.definition: To be a perfect example of a class or quality; to typify or embody something.
meanings.example: Her response to the crisis epitomised the calm, decisive leadership that the organisation desperately needed.

meanings.definition: To apply or bring to bear (a force, influence, or quality); to put into vigorous action.
meanings.example: The union exerted considerable pressure on management to improve safety conditions on the factory floor.

meanings.definition: To make a physical or mental effort; to strive.
meanings.example: She exerted herself tirelessly during the campaign, speaking at multiple events each day.

meanings.definition: Still in existence; surviving, especially of old documents or biological species.
meanings.example: Only three extant manuscripts of the medieval text have been identified by scholars.

meanings.definition: Characteristic of the belief that all events are predetermined and inevitable; accepting that nothing can change what will happen.
meanings.example: His fatalistic attitude toward the climate crisis frustrated colleagues who believed urgent action was still possible.

meanings.definition: Showing resignation to events as inevitable, often in the face of hardship or adversity.
meanings.example: A fatalistic acceptance of poverty had been instilled in the community over generations of failed reform.

meanings.definition: To lose or be deprived of (a right, privilege, or possession) as a penalty for doing or failing to do something.
meanings.example: By missing the deadline, they forfeited their right to appeal the original decision.

meanings.definition: To give up or relinquish something as a result of a fault, offence, or failure to comply.
meanings.example: The team forfeited the match after fielding an ineligible player in violation of competition rules.

meanings.definition: To encourage or promote the development of something, especially something desirable.
meanings.example: The mentorship programme was designed to foster a culture of continuous learning within the organisation.

meanings.definition: To bring up and care for a child that is not one's own, typically under a formal arrangement.
meanings.example: The couple fostered several children over the years before officially adopting one of them.

meanings.definition: To shock or excite someone into taking action; to stimulate someone into activity.
meanings.example: The documentary galvanised thousands of viewers into lobbying their representatives for climate legislation.

meanings.definition: To coat iron or steel with a protective layer of zinc to prevent rusting.
meanings.example: The steel frames of the bridge were galvanised before installation to ensure long-term corrosion resistance.

meanings.definition: Fluent and voluble but insincere or lacking depth; superficially smooth and plausible.
meanings.example: The politician's glib assurances did little to reassure constituents who had lived through years of broken promises.

meanings.definition: Speaking or spoken without careful consideration or depth of thought.
meanings.example: His glib dismissal of the report's concerns suggested he had not read it thoroughly.

meanings.definition: To keep a thought, feeling, or secret in one's mind, especially over a long period.
meanings.example: She had harboured resentment toward her former employer for years, which made moving on difficult.

meanings.definition: To give shelter or refuge to someone, especially someone accused of a crime.
meanings.example: The authorities suspected that local sympathisers were harbouring members of the fugitive group.

meanings.definition: To damage or weaken the strength, quality, or functioning of something.
meanings.example: Long-term exposure to loud noise can seriously impair hearing, even without causing acute pain.

meanings.definition: To diminish the value, effectiveness, or quality of something.
meanings.example: The unexpected resignation of two key executives impaired the company's ability to execute its strategy.

meanings.definition: To force (an unwanted or onerous thing) on someone or something; to establish by authority.
meanings.example: The sanctions imposed by the international community were designed to compel compliance with the treaty.

meanings.definition: To take advantage of someone by making unreasonable demands or burdening them.
meanings.example: He was reluctant to impose on his colleagues by asking them to cover for him again.

meanings.definition: Done without being planned, organised, or rehearsed; spontaneous.
meanings.example: The minister delivered an impromptu speech outside the building after the announcement was made.

meanings.definition: A short piece of music, especially a solo piano composition, of a free and informal character.
meanings.example: Schubert's impromptus are admired for their lyrical spontaneity and structural elegance.

meanings.definition: Not leading to a clear conclusion or definitive result; failing to settle a point at issue.
meanings.example: After three days of negotiations, the talks ended inconclusively, with both parties maintaining their positions.

meanings.definition: To provoke or intensify (strong feelings, especially negative ones) in a person or situation.
meanings.example: The leaked documents inflamed public opinion and made a political resolution far more difficult.

meanings.definition: To cause (a part of the body) to become hot, red, swollen, or painful; to produce inflammation.
meanings.example: The infection inflamed the surrounding tissue, requiring an extended course of antibiotics.

meanings.definition: To give a false or misleading account of the nature, facts, or intentions of something.
meanings.example: The report accused the company of misrepresenting its environmental record in advertising materials.

meanings.definition: To serve as a poor or inadequate representative of something.
meanings.example: The sample misrepresented the broader population by overweighting urban respondents.

meanings.definition: Full of danger or risk; fraught with serious hazard.
meanings.example: The rescue team undertook the perilous mission of extracting survivors from the collapsed mine shaft.

meanings.definition: To formally forbid something by law, rule, or authority; to prevent from happening.
meanings.example: The new regulations prohibit the use of single-use plastics in all food-service establishments.

meanings.definition: To make something impossible; to prevent or preclude by physical conditions.
meanings.example: The dense fog prohibit any takeoffs or landings at the airport for several hours.

meanings.definition: To drive or push forward with force; to cause something to move in a particular direction.
meanings.example: The rocket engines propel the spacecraft at sufficient velocity to escape Earth's gravitational pull.

meanings.definition: To cause someone to do something or to be driven into a particular situation.
meanings.example: The scandal propelled the minister to resign within 48 hours of the story breaking.

meanings.definition: To summarise and restate the main points of something that has been said or written.
meanings.example: The chairman recapitulated the key decisions taken during the board meeting before closing the session.

meanings.definition: To take the place of a person, thing, or system that is no longer in use or no longer as effective; to replace.
meanings.example: Digital streaming has largely superseded physical media for music and film consumption.

meanings.definition: To set aside or cause to be ineffective by taking a superior position or authority.
meanings.example: The new legislation supersedes all previous regulations governing data privacy in the jurisdiction.
